retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Carbonate -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium is naturally present in the body. Most of the body's stored calcium is within the bones and teeth as hydroxyapatite. The rest of the body's calcium is in blood, extracellular fluid, muscle, and other body tissues. [19] In the blood, 40% of calcium is bound to albumin, 13% binds to an anion, like phosphate and lactate, and the majority, 47%, is free, unbound, ionized calcium (Ca 2+ ). The amount of ionized calcium found in the blood is essential and tightly regulated by homeostatic controls because it is physiologically active and plays a vital role in the cellular processes of excitable tissues, such as blood vessels, muscles, glands, and nerves. [1] Calcium carbonate has three separate mechanisms of action that have pharmacologic effects. Calcium affects the stomach, small intestine, and blood. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: When calcium concentrations are low in the cytosol, the PMCA pump is in the resting position, elicited by the blocking of the phosphorylation site by the C-terminal auto-inhibitory domain. A calcium-sensing protein called cAM binds to calcium when concentrations of cytosolic calcium increase. This causes a conformational change driven by the interaction of Ca-cAM and the autoinhibitory site, which dissociates from the active site. Calcium is transported to the extracellular environment when it ATP-phosphorylates a conserved aspartate residue. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Calcium -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In serum, calcium exists in 3 forms: protein-bound, ionized (free), and complexed (chelated). Protein-bound calcium, which accounts for 40% of the serum calcium, cannot be used by tissues. Albumin and globulin are the primary calcium-binding proteins in the serum whereas calmodulin is the primary calcium-binding protein in the cell. Chelated calcium, which accounts for 9% of the serum calcium, allows calcium to be absorbed by various tissues or carried between parts of the body. Serum calcium is often chelated into the ionic complexes of calcium phosphate, calcium carbonate, and calcium oxalate. Finally, free calcium, which makes up 51% of the serum calcium, is utilized by the body to maintain physiologic functions. If the serum calcium concentration exceeds the 8.8 to 10.4 mg/dL, then the body is considered in a state of calcium toxicity. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Parathyroid -- Related Testing -- Ionized Serum Calcium
retrieved_docs.content: Ionized calcium, or free calcium, is the biologically active form of calcium necessary for many functions, including cell signaling, neurotransmission, and muscle contraction. Normal levels range from 4.64 to 5.28 mg/dL. Blood pH can affect ionized calcium levels by changing the binding affinity of albumin for positively charged anions. At low pH, an abundance of hydrogen ions effectively competes with free calcium for binding sites on negatively charged albumin, thus increasing free calcium in the serum.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Carbonate -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium carbonate works in the small intestines as a phosphate binder and drug chelator. In individuals with hyperphosphatemia or overdose, calcium will bind to form an insoluble compound blocking dietary phosphate or excess drug absorption and excreting it in feces. Additionally, calcium carbonate, used as a calcium supplement, also acts in the small intestine by chelating with oxalate to prevent absorption and renal calculi formation. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Carbonate -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: As an antacid, calcium carbonate neutralizes gastric acid by acting as a buffer in the stomach's acidic environment. When CaCO 3 enters the stomach, it dissociates into ionized calcium (Ca 2+ ) and a carbonate anion (CO 3 2- ). The carbonate anion will then bind to the free protons (H+) found in the stomach to increase the pH by decreasing the concentration of hydrogen ions. By increasing the pH in the stomach, pepsin, bile acids, and the toxins of Helicobacter pylori become inhibited. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Molecular Level -- Calcium
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium is an alkaline earth metal cation found in the form of Ca2+. Calcium is a critical divalent cation in intracellular and extracellular fluid. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Calcium -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium is an essential element that serves an important role in skeletal mineralization. More than 99% of the calcium in the body is stored in bone as hydroxyapatite. Calcium in this form provides skeletal strength as well as a reservoir for calcium to be released into the serum.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The NCX does not use the energy from ATP directly. Instead, its energy derives from the sodium concentration gradient, which is used to move calcium against its concentration gradient. This process is called secondary active transport.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Calcium -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Ionized calcium plays an important function in muscle contraction.  Skeletal muscle function is governed by an action potential that releases calcium stored in the sarcoplasmic reticulum. This calcium then binds to tropomyosin and allows for the interaction of myosin and actin in the sarcomere, leading to muscle contraction. In smooth muscle, second messenger systems trigger the release of calcium from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. Additionally, ligand-gated and voltage-gated calcium channels on the smooth muscle membrane allow for extracellular calcium to enter the cell. Calmodulin binds calcium ions and activates myosin light chain kinase to phosphorylase the myosin head, which then binds actin and causes smooth muscle contraction. Cardiac muscle is governed both by action potentials and extracellular calcium influx. The action potential triggers an inward flow of calcium that potentiates additional calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. The contraction of one cardiac muscle cell is communicated to adjacent cells through intercalated disks, thus allowing for the synchronized contraction of cardiac muscle. It is through this mechanism that calcium is used to stabilize the cardiac cell membrane against depolarization in severe hyperkalemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: When calcium concentration in the endoplasmic reticulum is low, this is sensed by the EF-hand domain on STIM. The release of calcium from the EF-Hand domain promotes its oligomerization into dimers and more complex oligomers due to the exposure of hydrophobic surfaces. STIM then redistributes via diffusion into clusters to sites near the plasma membrane at junctional sites that form between the PM and ER membranes. [37] Conformational changes in STIM mediate this process. [38] [37] At these membrane junctions, when STIM and Orai become close enough to each other, STIM binds to Orai, leading to the formation of a calcium channel called a calcium release-activated channel (CRAC), which allows the entry of calcium from an extracellular milieu into the endoplasmic reticulum. [40] [39] Calcium then flows through the channel, and once the store becomes refilled, the two proteins unbind from each other and diffuse away from the junctional sites back to their resting position. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Calcium
retrieved_docs.content: 99% of the calcium in the body is in bone and teeth as a structural component. The remaining calcium in the body is found in intracellular and extracellular spaces and plays key roles in innervation, muscle contraction, blood coagulation, hormone secretion, and intracellular adhesion. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Carbonate -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Lastly, calcium carbonate also works in the blood to treat or prevent negative calcium balance seen in low serum calcium conditions. [1] After being absorbed in the small intestines actively with the help of vitamin D and passively vi-a diffusion, ionized calcium circulates in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Calcium Channels -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: SERCA and PMCA belong to a group of ion pumps called P-type ATPases. P-type ATPases exist in two conformational states: E1 and E2. In the E1 conformation, the pump exhibits a high affinity for calcium on the cytoplasmic side of the cell. In the E2 conformational state, the transporter’s affinity for calcium rapidly decreases and subsequently leads to the release of calcium to the other side of the membrane. [42] Calcium ATPase transporters use the energy directly from ATP to pump calcium against its concentration gradient. This is known as primary active transport. [11] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and calcium phosphate dibasic are both classified as calcium compounds. As members of the broader calcium compounds category, they share the fundamental property of containing calcium ions, yet they are applied differently in medical practice. Calcium acetate is frequently used as a phosphate binder for patients with chronic kidney disease, helping to control serum phosphate levels, while calcium phosphate dibasic serves as a calcium source in nutritional supplements and supports bone health. Recognizing that both agents belong to the calcium compounds group aids clinicians in understanding their chemical characteristics and therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium compounds constitute a class of chemical substances that contain calcium, and this class includes calcium glycerophosphate, calcium acetate, and calcium oxide. Calcium glycerophosphate is a calcium‑phosphate salt that can serve as a source of both minerals. Calcium acetate is commonly employed as a phosphate‑binding agent in patients with renal failure to reduce serum phosphate levels. Calcium oxide, also known as quicklime, can be used as a calcium supplement or antacid in various medical contexts. All three substances are categorized under the broader term “Calcium Compounds,” reflecting their shared elemental composition while each possesses distinct chemical properties and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2160
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium compounds encompass several specific calcium salts, including kalcium‑glycerolfosfát, calcium acetate, and calcium phosphate dibasic. Each of these entities is classified as a type of calcium compound, sharing the fundamental chemical characteristic of containing calcium ions bound to various anionic groups. Kalcium‑glycerolfosfát represents a calcium‑glycerol‑phosphate complex, calcium acetate is the calcium salt of acetic acid, and calcium phosphate dibasic is a calcium salt of phosphoric acid. Recognizing these substances as members of the broader calcium compound category highlights their related pharmacologic and nutritional roles, such as providing calcium supplementation, acting as phosphate binders, or serving as buffering agents in clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate are both classified as calcium compounds, indicating that each belongs to the broader category of calcium‑containing substances. As members of this class, they share the fundamental chemical characteristic of providing bioavailable calcium and interacting with phosphate metabolism. Recognizing calcium acetate and tricalcium phosphate as calcium compounds helps clarify their roles in medical nutrition, calcium supplementation, and the management of bone health and phosphate balance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 148
retrieved_docs.content: activity, phosphate chelation, inhibition of bone resorption) and their synergistic inclusion with iron, calcium, other vitamins and trace minerals underpin their therapeutic, preventive and formulation‑stabilizing roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium Compounds represent a class of calcium‑containing chemical substances that include several distinct calcium salts. Among these are calcium glycerophosphate, which is a calcium‑based glycerophosphate compound, calcium acetate, a calcium salt of acetic acid, and tricalcium phosphate, a calcium phosphate used in various medical and nutritional contexts. All three entities share calcium as a central component, linking them together as members of the broader category of calcium compounds that are relevant for applications such as phosphate binding, calcium supplementation, and bone health management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2103
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium acetate is classified as a calcium compound, reflecting an “isa” relationship that places calcium acetate within the broader category of calcium compounds. This categorization indicates that calcium acetate shares the chemical characteristics of calcium compounds and informs its use in medical applications, such as serving as a phosphate binder in patients with renal impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2164
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium glubionate, calcium lactate, and calcium levulinate are all classified as calcium supplements. Each of these compounds provides elemental calcium and is used to correct calcium deficiency, support bone mineralization, and maintain overall calcium homeostasis in patients who require supplemental calcium intake. By belonging to the same therapeutic class of calcium supplements, they share similar clinical indications despite differences in their chemical forms and bioavailability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 61
retrieved_docs.content: a safety consideration. Mineral supplements form a broad therapeutic class that encompasses calcium, potassium, zinc, boron, phosphorus, manganese, molybdenum, selenium and related compounds. Calcium‑based agents listed as mineral supplements include calcium oxide, calcium protein preparations, calcium glubionate, calcium gluceptate, calcium lactate, calcium citrate (citrát vápenatý), calcium hydroxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, tricalcium phosphate, calcium‑glycerophosphate and calcium‑levulinate. Calcium supplements are classified as dietary supplements and also as calcium compounds, which include calcium acetate, calcium gluceptate, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, calcium hydroxide, tricalcium phosphate, calcium‑glycerophosphate and calcium glubionate. Calcium antacids and combination products such as ascorbic acid + calcium illustrate therapeutic applications, and calcium citrate is noted as a food additive together with methylated pectin. Replacement preparations

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phosphate is classified among the drugs used for treatment of hypercalcemia, indicating its role in managing elevated calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2353
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium protein preparations and calcium lactate are both classified as mineral supplements, representing specific types of calcium‑containing mineral supplement products used to support nutritional needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium glubionate and calcium lactate are both classified as mineral supplements, representing specific calcium‑containing compounds used to augment dietary calcium intake. As members of the broader mineral supplements category, these agents provide bioavailable calcium for patients requiring supplementation, supporting bone health and metabolic functions that depend on adequate calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2904
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium protein preparations and calcium gluceptate are both classified as mineral supplements, indicating that each of these specific products falls under the broader category of mineral supplement therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1903
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified within the functional group “hormones by function.” It is employed therapeutically to treat نقص كالسيوم الدم (hypocalcemia, a deficiency of calcium in the blood). Parathormon has a physiological relationship with kalcitonin (calcitonin); the two hormones interact to regulate calcium homeostasis, often exerting opposing effects to maintain appropriate serum calcium levels. Understanding the role of parathormon in calcium deficiency treatment and its interplay with kalcitonin is essential for effective management of disorders of calcium metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2290
retrieved_docs.content: Bulk compounding chemicals are a class of substances used in pharmaceutical preparation, and within this class calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate are identified as specific members. Calcium compounds represent another category of calcium‑based substances, comprising calcium acetate, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate. Consequently, calcium oxide, calcium phosphate dibasic, and tricalcium phosphate are recognized both as bulk compounding chemicals and as calcium compounds, highlighting their dual role in formulation processes and calcium chemistry. This overlap underscores the importance of these agents in both compounding practices and calcium‑related therapeutic applications.
